title: The News Habit Survey
description: A short survey about how you read and share news. There are no right or wrong answers — just pick what you'd actually do.

questions.question: You're scrolling and see a headline that matches something you've believed for a long time. What do you do?
options: Share it — it confirms what I already know to be true
Read the article before sharing
Check who published it and when
Look for a second source covering the same thing
questions.explanation: When a headline confirms existing beliefs, that's exactly when your guard should go up — not down. This is called confirmation bias. The most reliable instinct is to look for independent corroboration, not just read the article from the same source.

questions.question: A friend sends you a voice note saying 'I heard that [public figure] said something really controversial'. What's your next move?
options: Ask which outlet reported it
Search for the actual quote or video clip
Trust my friend — they wouldn't make it up
Share it with a 'is this true?' caption
questions.explanation: Second-hand claims — even from trusted people — are the most common way misinformation spreads. Your friend genuinely believes it. The only reliable move is finding the primary source: the actual quote, clip, or official statement. Paraphrases mutate with every retelling.

questions.question: You read two articles about the same government policy. One says it will help millions. The other says it will hurt millions. What do you conclude?
options: One of them is lying
The truth is probably somewhere in the middle
They may both be selectively accurate — I need to know who each outlet's audience is
I go with whichever outlet I usually trust
questions.explanation: Both articles can use real statistics and still mislead — by choosing which facts to highlight and which to omit. 'The truth is in the middle' sounds wise but is actually a lazy shortcut. The better question is: what is each outlet's incentive, and what are they not telling me?

questions.question: An article ends with 'experts say more research is needed'. How does that make you feel about the story?
options: Frustrated — just tell me the answer
Suspicious — they're hiding something
Reassured — that's honest journalism acknowledging uncertainty
Indifferent — I skip those parts
questions.explanation: Acknowledging uncertainty is a sign of credibility, not weakness. The most dangerous articles are the ones that sound completely certain. Science and policy are genuinely complicated — any outlet that never says 'we don't know yet' is simplifying for engagement, not informing you.

questions.question: A breaking news alert uses the words 'MASSIVE', 'SHOCKING' and three exclamation marks. Before reading further, what do you notice?
options: Nothing unusual — all breaking news looks like this
It's probably important if they're emphasising it that much
The language is designed to trigger an emotional reaction before I've read a single fact
I check if it's from a verified account first
questions.explanation: Caps, superlatives, and exclamation marks are emotional triggers — they activate your stress response before your critical thinking. This is deliberate. Once you're emotionally activated, you're less likely to question the content. Noticing the technique is the first step to resisting it.

questions.question: You notice that every news story about a particular political party on one channel is negative, and every story on another channel is positive. What does this tell you?
options: One channel is right and one is wrong
Both channels are selectively covering events to fit a narrative
The party must be controversial
I should watch both channels equally to get the full picture
questions.explanation: Consistent one-directional coverage — whether positive or negative — is a strong signal of narrative-driven journalism rather than event-driven journalism. Watching both channels equally doesn't fix this; it just exposes you to two competing narratives. The fix is looking for coverage that includes inconvenient facts for its own side.

questions.question: An article cites 'sources close to the matter' without naming them. How much weight do you give this?
options: Full weight — journalists protect sources for good reasons
Some weight — it's a lead worth following up, not a confirmed fact
No weight — if they can't name the source it didn't happen
It depends on whether I like the conclusion
questions.explanation: Anonymous sourcing is legitimate journalism — whistleblowers exist. But it should lower your confidence, not eliminate it or confirm it. Treat unnamed sources as a signal to look for corroboration, not as proof. The danger is when readers treat 'sources say' as equivalent to 'it is confirmed'.

questions.question: A statistic in a headline says 'Crime up 200% in the city'. What's the first question you ask?
options: Which city?
200% from what baseline — and over what time period?
Which party is in charge there?
That sounds serious, I'll share it as a warning
questions.explanation: Percentages without a baseline are almost meaningless. If there were 1 incident last year and 3 this year, that's 200% — but it's also statistically noise. The most common way to mislead with true numbers is to choose a starting point that makes a change look dramatic. Always ask: compared to what, and over how long?
